_comment: The Orc model manifest v1. This file is compiled into OrchestratorSetup.exe as an embedded resource (EmbeddedResources.ReadManifestJson()) -- despite manifest_url below, there is no live HTTP fetch of it at runtime. Editing this file and pushing to master only takes effect on the NEXT OrchestratorSetup.exe build+release; the already-published installer keeps whatever was embedded when it was compiled. (manifest_url is only consulted by future tooling, if any is built to actually fetch it live -- it is not consulted today.) The llama.cpp/Ollama runtime URLs ARE resolved live via GitHub's releases API at install time (see LlamaCppResolver.cs); only this manifest itself is build-time-baked.

app._comment: The main app, published as a self-contained single-file binary per OS (Avalonia since v1.9.0 -- WPF deleted). The installer downloads this before anything else. OS-keyed as of docs/MULTI_OS_RELEASE_SPEC.md Phase B -- InstallOrchestrator.ResolveAppUrl picks the right key via OperatingSystem.IsWindows()/IsMacOS()/IsLinux(), same pattern PlatformInstaller.Resolve() already uses. Each download_url points at GitHub's evergreen 'latest' release-asset alias, so it always resolves to whatever release is currently marked Latest -- no installer rebuild needed when a new app version ships. size_mb is only a progress-bar estimate (the real download uses the HTTP response's actual Content-Length) -- kept roughly accurate for UX, not load-bearing. linux key intentionally absent -- release.yml has no Linux publish job yet (Phase A), so there is no real asset to point at; ResolveAppUrl falls back to empty/manifest-fallback-URL behavior there until that lands.

llama_cpp._comment: Static fallback URLs only. The installer calls the GitHub releases API first to get the actual latest asset URLs dynamically (LlamaCppResolver.cs, OS+arch-aware as of MULTI_OS_RELEASE_SPEC.md Phase D) — these values are only used if that API call fails. OS-keyed as of 2026-06-24 (mirroring the 'app' key's windows/macos/no-linux-yet pattern from Phase B) -- the previous flat, non-OS-keyed table couldn't represent that the SAME variant name (e.g. 'cpu') has a DIFFERENT real filename per OS; InstallOrchestrator.BuildRuntimeFallbackUrl/GetRuntimeSizeBytes picked whatever was under that flat key regardless of the running OS, so a Linux box whose GitHub API call failed would have silently received a Windows .zip as its 'fallback'. Filenames/sizes verified live (`gh api repos/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases/latest`, 2026-06-24) for every OS below, not guessed -- this also caught the existing Windows vulkan/avx2/cpu entries already being stale (missing the 'bNNNN-' build-number segment every non-cudart asset actually carries; this snapshot is only ever as fresh as its last manual edit, same staleness risk this comment's first sentence already accepts). The 'cudart-' prefix on Windows CUDA variants means the CUDA runtime DLLs are bundled so no separate CUDA install is needed -- llama.cpp keeps 'cudart-' filenames free of the build-number segment, unlike every other asset. macOS ships one unified 'metal' build per architecture (no CUDA/Vulkan-equivalent split needed -- Metal is always available). Linux real coverage today is vulkan/cpu only -- no plain CUDA build exists in the current release (ROCm/SYCL/OpenVINO exist but this manifest's variant taxonomy has no slot for them yet); cuda11/cuda12 intentionally have no linux key at all, not a null placeholder, so a lookup miss is the correct fail-closed signal rather than a fabricated empty value.

hardware_selection._comment: Documentation only -- not parsed/consumed by any C# code today (HardwareDetector/MacPlatformInstaller/LinuxPlatformInstaller each pick RuntimeVariant directly, not via this map). Kept as a reference for the actual decision logic, not a load-bearing config surface.

ollama._comment: Ollama installer download. Runs silently by default on Windows.

models._comment: NVIDIA's official recommended model for TheOrc. Optimised for NVIDIA GPUs, strong agentic tool use, natively available on Ollama. Highlighted as NVIDIA partner pick. Recommended for NVIDIA systems with 4–8 GB VRAM.
models.name: NVIDIA Nemotron Mini 4B
models.description: NVIDIA's own recommended agent model. Built for agentic tool use and instruction following. Runs efficiently on NVIDIA GPUs from 4 GB VRAM up. Pull via Ollama or download GGUF.

models._comment: Google Gemma 4 — latest generation flagship model from Google DeepMind. Excellent instruction following, coding, and reasoning. Highlighted as Google partner pick. Best for NVIDIA 8–12 GB VRAM systems. OLLAMA-ONLY: the HuggingFace GGUF repo is gated behind Google license acceptance and returns 401 for unauthenticated users. url is intentionally null — pull via Ollama instead.
models.name: Google Gemma 4 12B
models.description: Google DeepMind's latest Gemma 4 generation. Outstanding at instruction following, coding tasks, and multi-step reasoning. Install via Ollama — the GGUF download requires a HuggingFace account and Google license acceptance.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 1.5B
models.description: Ultra-light coder. Best choice for CPU-only machines or GPUs below 4 GB. Handles simple edits and single-file tasks well.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 3B
models.description: Solid entry-level coder. Fits comfortably in 4 GB VRAM. Good for straightforward coding tasks and Q&A.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B  ★ Balanced
models.description: The sweet spot. Excellent coding quality for multi-step tasks, strong tool use, fast responses. Recommended for 6–8 GB VRAM.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B  (Q8 full)
models.description: Near-lossless 7B. Best quality in the 7B class. Ideal for 8–12 GB VRAM users who want maximum accuracy.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 14B  ★ Recommended
models.description: Handles complex multi-file tasks, architecture discussions, and refactoring with ease. Best pick for 12–16 GB VRAM.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B  (Q3 compressed)
models.description: Full 32B class in a lighter package. Outstanding reasoning and large-codebase understanding. For 16–20 GB VRAM.

models.name: Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B  ★ Best Available
models.description: The best locally-runnable coding model. Elite at architecture, debugging, and large-scale refactoring. Requires 24 GB VRAM.

models._comment: Security-specific alternative. Fewer RLHF restrictions — designed for agentic/research use. Recommended when the Security/Pentest profile is selected.
models.name: Hermes 4 14B  (Security)
models.description: NousResearch — purpose-built agentic model. Fewer restrictions than standard models, excellent tool use, designed for security research and red-team workflows.

models._comment: Game dev / systems alternative. Stronger on C++, HLSL/GLSL, and low-level algorithm reasoning than base Qwen models.
models.name: DeepSeek Coder V2 Lite  (Game/Systems)
models.description: Specialised for C++, game engine code, shader languages, and systems-level algorithms. Alternative pick for Game Dev and Systems/Embedded profiles.

models._comment: Compact high-quality model for UI/UX and mobile profiles where task complexity is moderate but speed matters.
models.name: Phi-4 Mini  (UI/Mobile)
models.description: Microsoft's compact but surprisingly capable model. Fast responses, good at structured output. Great for UI component generation and mobile app patterns.

models._comment: Uncensored chat models (Cognitive Computations' Dolphin line) -- continued fine-tunes with RLHF refusal training removed, not auto-recommended by hardware tier (opt-in only). Smallest of the three, CPU-capable.
models.name: Dolphin 3.0 Qwen2.5 3B
models.description: Lightweight uncensored chat model. A Qwen2.5-3B continued fine-tune with refusal training removed. Runs on CPU.

models._comment: Uncensored chat -- mid-tier, 6-10 GB VRAM. Continued fine-tune of Mistral Nemo 12B with refusal training removed.
models.name: Dolphin 2.9.3 Mistral Nemo 12B
models.description: Mid-tier uncensored chat model. A continued fine-tune of Mistral Nemo 12B with refusal behavior removed via training. Strong instruction following for its size.

models._comment: Uncensored chat -- high-end, 18+ GB VRAM. SUBSTITUTED: the originally-requested 'Dolphin 3.0 Yi-34B' does not exist on Hugging Face -- this is the closest current equivalent (2.9.3, 32k-context variant). File is 16.65 GB, larger than a 16GB-tier estimate would suggest -- vram_min_gb set to 18 to reflect this.
models.name: Dolphin 2.9.3 Yi 1.5 34B (32k)
models.description: Largest uncensored chat model offered -- strongest reasoning of the three, requires a high-end GPU. A continued fine-tune of Yi-1.5 34B with refusal training removed.

vram_to_model_id._comment: Generic (non-NVIDIA) VRAM tier map. NVIDIA hardware uses nvidia_vram_to_model_id instead, which prefers NVIDIA Nemotron + Google Gemma 4.

nvidia_vram_to_model_id._comment: NVIDIA-specific tier map. Prefers NVIDIA Nemotron Mini (4–7 GB) and Google Gemma 4 12B (8–11 GB) on NVIDIA hardware. Evaluated by the installer when vendor == 'nvidia'.

profile_model_overrides._comment: When the user selects these profiles AND has sufficient VRAM, the installer suggests this model INSTEAD OF the VRAM-tier default. The user can still choose the Qwen model.
